content.full_text:
Re:
F atn P oltrtcAL P RACTTcEs C or'rrrarssroN
.128 J Street r Suite 620 o Sacramento. CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 r Fax (916) 322-0886
January l7,2OO8
Douglas C. Holland
Woodruff, Spradlin & Smart
555 Anton Blvd., Slrite 1200
Costa Mesa, CA 92626-7 670
Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-07-176
Dear Mr. Holland:
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of City Councilmember
Jim Palmer regarding the gift and campaign provisions of the Political Reform Act (the
"Act").' Please note that the Commission does not act as the finder of fact when it
renders advice. In addition, nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate any
conduct that has already taken place. (ln re Oglesblt (1975) t FPPC Ops. 71.)
QUESTTONS
1. Are donations that Jim Palmer, a city councilmember and the President of a
faith-based charitable organization, collects on behalf of and for the benefit of his
employer, considered gifts when made at his behest?
2. Are these donations contributions to the councilmember?
3. Are the donations reportable "behested payments" if they are $5,000 or more?
CONCLUSION
l. Under these circumstances, donations that Mr. Palmer solicits for his
employer, the nonprofit organization, will not constitute gifts to him.
2. Under these circumstances, donations to his employer will not constitute
contributions to Mr. Palmer.
I The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 8 l0(X) through 9101,1. All
statutory ref'erences are to the Government Code. unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair
Politisal Practices Commission are contained in Sections l8l l0 through 18997 of Title 2 of the Califbrnia
Code of Regulations. All regulatory ret'erences are to Title 2. Division 6 of the Califirrnia Code of
Regulations. unless otherwise indicated.


File No. A-07-176
Page No.2
3. A donation to the organization is reportable by Mr. Palmer, as described
below, as a charitable contribution if it is $5,000 or more in the aggregate from the same
source in the same calendar year and is made at the behest of Mr. Palmer.
FACTS
You are a city attorney for the City of Tustin. You request advice on behalf of
Jim Palmer, a city councilmember for the City of Tustin, and a de facto member of the
Tustin Community Redevelopment Agency. Mr. Palmer is also the President of the
Orange County Rescue Mission, Inc. (the "Rescue Mission"), a non-profit, organized
under section 501(c)(3) of the Internal Revenue code.
The Rescue Mission is a faith-based non-profit that provides homeless recovery
services to the Orange County homeless population. Mr. Palmer works full-time for the
Rescue Mission as the President of the organization, as he has for the past 15 years. Mr.
Palmer receives a salary from the Rescue Mission and serves at the pleasure of the Board
of Directors. Mr. Palmer is the "administrative head" of the organization under the
direction and control of the Board of Directors.
Among his other responsibilities, Mr. Palmer fundraises on behalf of the Rescue
Mission, although his efforts are limited to, as listed in your request for advice:
"a) occasionally speaking to groups of potential donors or answering questions presented
by a potential individual donor interested in making a significant contribution, if
contacted directly or upon request; b) assisting in the developing and./or promotion of a
large capital campaign, and c) generally supervising the internal development of staff
who conduct most of the day-to-day fundraising activities."
While the Rescue Mission brings in hundreds of donations each year of $5,000 or
more, these are rarely made at the behest of Mr. Palmer through direct solicitations. The
organization receives funds from these private donations, as well as limited public
funding. The Rescue Mission combines these funds to pay for program funding and
administrative expenses, including Mr. Palmer's salary. Mr. palmer's salary and
performance reviews are not affected by the donations received.
On our January 3, 2008 telephone conversation, you provided more information
regarding Mr. Palmer's role in and the operations of the Rescue Mission. The Rescue
Mission's annual budget is over $16 million. Of that, the Rescue Mission receives 857o
through private donations, and the remaining L5vo through public grants.
Mr. Palmer's salary is "less than $200,000." For the sake of mathematical ease,
we've assumed a $200,000 salary for Mr. Palmer, and a $16 million annual budget for the
Rescue Mission. Using these figures, Mr. Palmer's salary represents l.25Vo of the
budget. You also informed us that Mr. Palmer's annual reviews are based on similar
criteria that similar organizations use to evaluate similar positions. Employee reviews do
not address the fundraising campaigns, and bonuses are not part of the Rescue Mission's
compensation package for this position.


File No. A-07-176
Page No. 3
Mr. Palmer has been a city councilmember since November, 2006. His status as a
candidate or elected official has not been, and will not be, mentioned in correlation with
requests for donations. Mr. Palmer does not solicit contributions for his campaign in
connection with any Rescue Mission fundraising.
ANALYSIS
Gifts
Section 82028(a) defines "gift" as a "payment that confers personal benefit on the
recipient, to the extent that consideration of equal or greater value is not received." We
have previously found, in the context of donations of money and personal property, that
because the donations the candidate solicited were given directly to the charitible entity,
they were not gifts to the candidate. (In re Nejedly,2 Fppc ops.45 (1976).) we have
also stated that California law looks to three factors when defining a gift: (1) donor's
intent; (2) delivery; and (3) donee's acceptance . (In re Russell,l FPPC Ops. 135 (t975);
In re Nejedly, supra.)
The facts you present indicate that Mr. Palmer has been in his current position of
President of the Rescue Mission for 15 years and a city councilmember since lOOe . m
his position for the Rescue Mission he is rarely involved in soliciting charitable donations
to that organization and when he does, he does not mention his official status as a city
councilmember. [n addition, his salary represents approximately L.25vo of the
organization's annual revenue, he is not paid based on a percentage of revenue the
organization raises, and the organization does not consider fundraising when conducting
annual performance reviews for Mr. Palmer or in setting his salary.
Based on these facts, donations to the Rescue Mission, while providing a benefit
to Mr. Palmer in the sense that they form the basis for the organization's budgit and
therefore pay his salary, do not appear to be given with the intent by the donors to
personally benefit Mr. Palmer. On this basis, we do not think that general donations to
the organization are gifts to him.
Please note that our conclusion on this point could change. For example, if a
particular donor made a large donation to the organization and the donor has tusiness
before Mr. Palmer in his official capacity or the donation is given with the intent that it be
used to increase Mr. Palmer's salary, and Mr. Palmer's salary increases as a foreseeable
result of the donation, we may conclude that the donation is a gift to him. Also, we may
conclude a particular donor is a source of a gift to Mr. Palmer where the donor's donation
to the organization constitutes a significant proportion of the funds used to pay his salary
or benefits. At this point, however, based upon the facts you present, *e "orclrde that
donations to the organization are not gifts to Mr. palmer.
Contributions
The Act defines a contribution as "a payment, a forgiveness of a loan, a payment
of a loan by a third party, or an enforceable promise to make a payment except to the
extent that full and adequate consideration is received, unless it is clear from the


File No. A-07-176
Page No.4
surrounding circumstances that it is not made for political purposes." (Section 82015(a).)
This section further explains that behested paymentsr are contributions unless the official
receives full and adequate consideration or that "it is clear from the surrounding
circumstances that the payment was made for purposes unrelated to his or her candidacy
for elective office." (ld.) The Act presumes that payments made "principally for
legislative, governmental, or charitable purposes" are neither gifts nor contributions.
(Section 820 I s(bX2XBXiii).)
As previously discussed, you indicate that Mr. Palmer has been employed with
the Rescue Mission for l5 years; he is currently the president of the organiiation and
among his other duties, he also does some fundraising for the Rescue Mission; he does
not mention his status as a public official when raising funds for his employer. Also, by
all appearances, the donations the Rescue Mission accepts at Mr. Palmei's behest are
charitable in nature, and are not spent for political purposes. Consequently, we conclude
that donations to the organization are not contributions to Mr. Palmer. This conclusion
could change, however, if the organization began using the donations to make campaign
contributions or other political expenditures.
"Behested Payment"
The Act states that when a payment is made at the behest of an elected official for
a legislative, governmental, or charitable purpose and the payment is not otherwise a gift
or contribution, the official must report the payment if, when combined with similar
payments by the same source during a calendar year the total of the payments is $5,000
or more. (Section 82015(bX2XBXiii).) Generally, a donation will be made "at the
behest" of Mr. Palmer if it is solicited, requested, or suggested by him or otherwise made
to the organization in cooperation, consultation, coordination with, or with the consent of,
Mr. Palmer. (See Regulation 18225.7(a).) Thus, if any donor makes a payment at Mr.
Palmer's behest for a legislative, governmental, or charitable purpose, Mr. palmer must
report those payments as provided in Section 82015(bX2XBXiii).3 We do not mean to
say that all donations made to the Rescue Mission must be reported. Rather, those
limited donations that equal or exceed $5,000 in the aggregate from the same source in a
calendar year and that are donated specifically at Mr. Palmer's behest must be reported.
t "Made at the behest ofl'generally means made under the control or at the direction of, in
cooperation, consultation. coordination, or concert with, at the request or suggestion of, or with the express,
prior consent of. (Regulation 18225.7.)
I In Mr. Palmer's case, the report must be filed with the city of Tustin within 30 days following
the date the payment meets or exceeds the $5,000 annual threshold for the donor. The repoit must contain
the name and address of the payor. amount of the payment, date or dates the payment or payments were
made' the name and address of the payee, a brief description of the form in which the payment was made
(or' if in-kind, the goods or services provided), and a description of the purpose tbr thi donation. The
reportisapublicrecordsubjecttocopyingandinspectionassetforinSectionSl008(a). 
(Section
820 rs(b)(2)(BXiii).)


File No. A-07-176
Page No. 5
Please be advised that we base our opinion on the specific, limited facts provided.
Different situations may yield different results.
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel

By: Heather M. Rowan
Counsel, Legal Division

sections.question:
1. Are donations that Jim Palmer, a city councilmember and the President of a
faith-based charitable organization, collects on behalf of and for the benefit of his
employer, considered gifts when made at his behest?
2. Are these donations contributions to the councilmember?
3. Are the donations reportable "behested payments" if they are $5,000 or more?
sections.conclusion:
l. Under these circumstances, donations that Mr. Palmer solicits for his
employer, the nonprofit organization, will not constitute gifts to him.
2. Under these circumstances, donations to his employer will not constitute
contributions to Mr. Palmer.
3. A donation to the organization is reportable by Mr. Palmer, as described
below, as a charitable contribution if it is $5,000 or more in the aggregate from the same
source in the same calendar year and is made at the behest of Mr. Palmer.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 1. Are donations that Jim Palmer, a city councilmember and the President of a
faith-based charitable organization, collects on behalf of and for the benefit of his
employer, considered gifts when made at his behest?
2. Are these donations contributions to the councilmember?
3. Are the donations reportable "behested payments" if they are $5,000 or more?

CONCLUSION: l. Under these circumstances, donations that Mr. Palmer solicits for his
employer, the nonprofit organization, will not constitute gifts to him.
2. Under these circumstances, donations to his employer will not constitute
contributions to Mr. Palmer.
3. A donation to the organization is reportable by Mr. Palmer, as described
below, as a charitable contribution if it is $5,000 or more in the aggregate from the same
source in the same calendar year and is made at the behest of Mr. Palmer.
